caveats: a: No public occupation mix or time-use study exists for NAICS 562219.
a: The code's treatment technologies vary widely, so exposure can differ materially by facility type.
a: The frozen l value exceeds one because of its stated wage, receipts, vintage, and harmonization construction and should not be interpreted as a literal controllable compensation share.
rho: Manufacturing and all-business surveys do not isolate waste treatment or realized labor substitution.
rho: Reported AI use includes chatbots and business functions that may not touch treatment production.
rho: Implementation feasibility is likely bimodal between simple outdoor sites and instrumented plants.
e: NAICS classification does not identify private control, recurring revenue, captive status, or normalized EBITDA.
e: The margin-bridged firm count uses a broad sector margin that may not fit composting and specialized treatment equally.
e: The boundary with materials recovery, collection, remediation, wastewater, and agricultural composting can be difficult in establishment records.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Waste-sector M&A totals are dominated by large and adjacent business models and provide no 562219 denominator.
s5: Environmental liabilities, permits, land-use approvals, and customer-contract assignment can prevent or delay a sale.
q: One Canadian composting company does not establish the contract mix of U.S. NAICS 562219.
q: Pricing form does not directly measure the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
q: Compost, recovered-product, and environmental-credit prices can transmit gains or losses independently of treatment productivity.
d5: The strongest demand evidence concerns organics and composting rather than the full heterogeneous code.
d5: EPA's tonnage statistics are not a five-year constant-price service forecast and use different reference years and facility populations.
d5: Food-waste prevention and on-site or community composting can reduce outsourced commercial throughput even when diversion rises.
o: The estimate combines materially different treatment processes with different staffing and self-service potential.
o: Decentralized or on-site organics processing may displace some external facility demand.
o: Automation can reduce on-site staffing while leaving legal and operational accountability with the same firm.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat gate and tipping fees, service agreements based on waste type, frequency, weight or volume, municipal or commercial processing contracts, combined collection fees, and secondary revenue from compost, recovered products, environmental attributes, or related management services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hazardous-waste treatment, solid-waste landfills, combustors and incinerators, sewer and sewage-treatment systems, materials recovery facilities primarily sorting recyclables, pure waste collection without treatment, captive internal facilities, public programs without a transferable operating business, and firms outside the lower-middle-market size band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators repeatedly providing external customers with nonhazardous waste treatment or disposal other than landfill, combustion, incineration, or sewer treatment, including commercial composting and other physical, chemical, or biological treatment facilities and combined local collection-and-treatment operations.

a.bridge: O*NET process-machine tasks and BLS treatment-plant operator duties are used as proxies for the mixed 562219 workforce. Instrument review, anomaly detection, dosing and process recommendations, logs, regulatory reports, scheduling, routing, billing, and maintenance planning are treated as AI-exposed; material loading, contamination removal, sampling, cleaning, equipment operation, repairs, and site inspection are treated as less exposed. These occupation proxies may overstate digital exposure for low-tech composting and understate it for sensor-rich industrial treatment. The supplied l value of 1.0835 is a vintage- and denominator-sensitive dataset anomaly and is not treated as a literal feasible labor-cost share.
caveats: No public occupation mix or time-use study exists for NAICS 562219.
The code's treatment technologies vary widely, so exposure can differ materially by facility type.
The frozen l value exceeds one because of its stated wage, receipts, vintage, and harmonization construction and should not be interpreted as a literal controllable compensation share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted staffing census and task diary across composting, industrial nonhazardous treatment, and combined collection-and-treatment firms, explicitly measuring already automated processes and contractor labor, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: Treatment facilities generate repeat operational, laboratory, customer, and compliance data that AI can structure and analyze, but most frontline work manipulates variable physical waste streams and equipment. Even computerized plants require manual operation during failures, sampling, maintenance, safety response, and licensed or accountable oversight. The estimate therefore assigns a meaningful but minority share of current wage-weighted work to not-yet-automated opportunity.

d5.bridge: EPA reports 66.2 million tons of wasted food from retail, food service, and residential sources in 2019 with only 5% composted, describes mandatory recycling and composting programs that require services, and finds that existing organics facilities process about 28.3 million tons while additional infrastructure is needed. These organics-specific indicators are used as a growth proxy for the broader 562219 service basket. They likely overstate growth for mature or non-organics treatment niches and may understate local growth where diversion mandates and landfill constraints are binding.
caveats: The strongest demand evidence concerns organics and composting rather than the full heterogeneous code.
EPA's tonnage statistics are not a five-year constant-price service forecast and use different reference years and facility populations.
Food-waste prevention and on-site or community composting can reduce outsourced commercial throughput even when diversion rises.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit real-revenue or throughput forecast decomposed into composting, anaerobic or biological processing, physical and chemical treatment, and combined collection-treatment, paired with state mandate implementation, permitted capacity, feedstock contracts, closures, and end-product demand, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Organics diversion, commercial composting mandates, landfill-methane priorities, and insufficient processing infrastructure create a credible growth path. Growth is constrained by contamination, siting opposition, land and transport costs, permitting delays, weak end markets, self-composting, prevention of food waste, and inconsistent enforcement. Because the code also contains treatment activities outside organics, the base is moderate rather than aggressive.

caveats: NAICS classification does not identify private control, recurring revenue, captive status, or normalized EBITDA.
The margin-bridged firm count uses a broad sector margin that may not fit composting and specialized treatment equally.
The boundary with materials recovery, collection, remediation, wastewater, and agricultural composting can be difficult in establishment records.
e.change_evidence: A record-level audit of the frozen 56 firms using Business Register descriptions, state permits, facility websites, customer contracts, ownership records, and normalized financials to code primary activity, external recurrence, control transferability, and EBITDA would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition centers on operating treatment and disposal facilities or combined collection and treatment, which normally creates recurring external-service revenue. Deductions cover public or captive operations, firms whose actual activity belongs in materials recovery or collection, nonrepeat project work, owner-dependent microbusinesses, and records whose margin-bridged size differs from normalized EBITDA. Eligibility is likely a majority of the frozen population but cannot be observed from public aggregates.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 562219 ($1,729,804,382) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 562219 ($1,702,171,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2099 (margins.json[562]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Environmental & Waste Services EBITDA/Sales 20.99%); revenue/employee $341,185 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 562219); class <5: 341 firms, avg revenue/firm $357,824 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $75,107 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,607,859 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $337,490 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,012,896 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $632,407 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,415,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,186,235 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $76,447,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,046,351 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,058,231 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,568,623 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 56

caveats: The estimate combines materially different treatment processes with different staffing and self-service potential.
Decentralized or on-site organics processing may displace some external facility demand.
Automation can reduce on-site staffing while leaving legal and operational accountability with the same firm.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement data measuring shifts to on-site treatment, community composting, alternative disposal, or software-only services, plus permit and insurer requirements for qualified on-site operators and evidence of commercially autonomous facilities, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Most surviving demand requires an accountable operator to accept and characterize waste, control contamination, run treatment equipment, manage biological or chemical conditions, sample outputs, maintain the site, and comply with permits. Software can self-serve scheduling, documentation, billing, and limited monitoring; generators can also prevent waste, compost on site, or redirect material to alternative pathways. Those substitutions reduce but do not eliminate the operator requirement for centralized commercial treatment.

q.bridge: SusGlobal's organic-composting filing provides a direct service-model example but from a Canadian small public company: revenue comes from tipping fees for delivered waste and compost sales, with service pricing varying by frequency, waste type, weight, volume, and regional market conditions. These per-load or per-ton arrangements can retain labor and process savings until prices reset, while competitive gate pricing, municipal tenders, commodity-linked output economics, and customer negotiation share benefits over time. The example may overstate merchant flexibility or understate fixed-contract protection in U.S. operators.
caveats: One Canadian composting company does not establish the contract mix of U.S. NAICS 562219.
Pricing form does not directly measure the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
Compost, recovered-product, and environmental-credit prices can transmit gains or losses independently of treatment productivity.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and invoice-level data from a representative panel showing revenue by fixed fee, per-ton tip fee, cost-plus, municipal tender, commodity rebate, and product sale, together with renewal cadence and post-productivity repricing, would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Treatment is typically priced for an accepted physical quantity or contracted service rather than for disclosed labor hours, so efficiency gains can accrue to the operator in the near term. Retention is moderated by competitive tipping fees, transport alternatives, pass-through clauses, output-price volatility, renewal repricing, and the need to reinvest savings in contamination control and compliance.

rho.bridge: NIST's manufacturing evidence on AI adoption, predictive maintenance, process monitoring, and barriers, plus Census all-business adoption by firm size, is applied to physical waste-treatment operations. The base assumes five-year expansion in sensor analytics, maintenance, documentation, and back-office workflows while discounting for small-firm capital limits, inconsistent input data, outdoor operations, legacy equipment, safety, cybersecurity, and environmental validation. Manufacturing adoption may overstate implementation at low-tech compost sites but understate it at sophisticated continuous treatment plants.
caveats: Manufacturing and all-business surveys do not isolate waste treatment or realized labor substitution.
Reported AI use includes chatbots and business functions that may not touch treatment production.
Implementation feasibility is likely bimodal between simple outdoor sites and instrumented plants.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews and system demonstrations from at least fifteen LMM 562219 operators, recording workflow coverage, validated hours saved, avoided downtime, deployment failures, override rates, and total integration cost, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Many candidate workflows use mature data and document tools rather than autonomous equipment, making partial implementation credible within five years. Broad capture will lag because contaminated and changing feedstocks make model transfer difficult, smaller firms lack data engineering capacity, and operators must preserve manual fallback and regulatory traceability.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intention for U.S. employer-business owners is the general anchor. Waste Dive reports sustained high waste and recycling M&A activity, including $10.9 billion of public-company acquisition spending in 2024, numerous tuck-ins, treatment facilities, and active pipelines; this supports an upward adjustment for industry buyer demand. The adjustment may overstate LMM 562219 transfers because the reported spending includes very large collection, landfill, recycling, hazardous, Canadian, and minority transactions, while permitted treatment assets can also be attractive scarce infrastructure.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Waste-sector M&A totals are dominated by large and adjacent business models and provide no 562219 denominator.
Environmental liabilities, permits, land-use approvals, and customer-contract assignment can prevent or delay a sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census linked to U.S. 562219 facility permits and the frozen size population, separating qualifying control sales from minority investments, asset purchases, internal reorganizations, public contract awards, and closures, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Strategic waste operators and infrastructure-backed buyers seek permitted facilities, local density, feedstock contracts, and technical capabilities, while independent owners face compliance and capital pressures. Not every planned transfer closes, and some transactions acquire routes or facilities without a separately qualifying control business, so the range remains broad.

principal_driver: Mandated organics diversion and repeat physical treatment fees can support data-assisted process and maintenance gains at scarce permitted facilities.
principal_weakness: Heterogeneous plant types, physical frontline work, permit and liability risk, and an anomalous compensation anchor make the labor opportunity and target universe unusually uncertain.

caveats: S1 is labeled as a 2022 NAICS source, but the reopened Census archive page is headed 1997 NAICS. The displayed 562219 definition supports the lens, so this is a citation-metadata issue rather than a score-changing defect.

The a and o assessments rely on adjacent process-machine and wastewater-operator duties rather than an observed 562219 staffing or time-use mix. The packet labels and bounds this limitation honestly, but facility heterogeneity remains substantial.

The rho base translates manufacturing and all-business AI adoption into five-year implementation at LMM waste-treatment sites. The sources establish adoption and relevant use cases and barriers, but not realized workflow coverage or labor substitution in 562219.

The s5 uplift above Gallup's broad 22% intention anchor is informed mainly by transactions and pipelines of large public waste companies across adjacent, hazardous, and Canadian activities. It indicates buyer appetite but supplies no LMM 562219 transfer denominator.

S8 establishes per-unit and contract-specific pricing but does not measure the discounted share of AI-enabled benefits retained by U.S. 562219 operators; q therefore remains a broad pricing-form proxy.
The d5 base is a disclosed inference from organics mandates, low composting penetration, and infrastructure need, not a five-year constant-price forecast for the full heterogeneous 562219 code.

sources_audited.note: The archived Census page supports the 562219 definition, exclusions, combined local collection-and-treatment activity, and compost-dump example. Its page header identifies the material as 1997 NAICS, not the packet's stated 2022 title, but this metadata mismatch does not alter the substantive boundary used.

O*NET documents loading, equipment operation, instrument monitoring, control adjustment, sampling, inspection, logging, cleaning, and impurity-removal tasks. It supports the stated task proxy, not a measured occupation mix for 562219.

BLS supports computerized monitoring, sampling, regulatory reporting, maintenance, manual fallback, licensing, emergency duties, and the projected 7% employment decline from 2024 to 2034. Wastewater treatment is adjacent to and excluded from the lens, as the packet discloses through its proxy treatment.

NIST reports 46% current manufacturer AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, and identifies predictive analytics, maintenance, process applications, data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system barriers.

Census reports December 2025-May 2026 all-business AI usage of 17%-20%, expected six-month usage of 20%-23%, and higher current adoption among larger firms. It also states the question covers AI in any business function, consistent with the packet's caveat.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The measure is an intention for employer businesses generally, not completed qualifying 562219 transfers.

Waste Dive reports $10.9 billion of 2024 acquisitions by five major public waste companies, numerous acquisitions and tuck-ins, treatment-related transactions, and active pipelines. The article is dominated by large, adjacent, Canadian, and hazardous-waste activity rather than LMM 562219 transactions.

The filing supports tipping-fee and compost revenue, contract-specific service pricing by frequency, waste type, weight, volume, and regional factors, plus physical processing, curing, and screening. It demonstrates one Canadian issuer's pricing form but does not measure retained AI benefits.

EPA explains that mandatory recycling and composting rules can require governments or contractors to provide services to all customers and can impose service-provider conditions. It establishes a policy mechanism, not a national throughput forecast.
EPA reports 66.2 million tons of wasted food from the stated sectors in 2019 and 5% composted, and describes facility scale, feedstock, equipment, and contamination considerations.

EPA reports about 28.3 million tons of organic waste processed, states that additional processing infrastructure is needed, and details feedstock contamination, equipment and capital, permitting, land, labor, transport, and market barriers.

run_meta.title: Other Nonhazardous Waste Treatment and Disposal

sources.supports: Defines 562219 as nonhazardous waste treatment or disposal facilities other than landfills, combustors, incinerators, and sewer treatment, including combined local collection and treatment and compost dumps, and distinguishes adjacent industries.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau — 2022 NAICS Sector 56 Definitions

sources.supports: Describes loading materials, process monitoring, control adjustment, sampling, inspection, logging, cleaning, and maintenance tasks used as a proxy for physical and chemical waste-treatment operations.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine — Separating, Filtering, Clarifying, Precipitating, and Still Machine Operators

sources.supports: Describes computerized monitoring alongside sampling, reporting, manual fallback, maintenance, safety, licensing, and emergency duties; projects 7% occupational decline from 2024 to 2034 as automation advances while skilled operators remain necessary.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Water and Wastewater Treatment Plant and System Operators

sources.supports: Reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use in two years; identifies predictive maintenance, process optimization, sensor analysis, and barriers including data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration.
sources.title: NIST Manufacturing Extension Partnership — The Rise of AI in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports nationally representative late-2025 to May-2026 business AI use of 17%-20%, expected near-term use of 20%-23%, and higher adoption among larger firms.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau — Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users

sources.supports: Reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in a fall 2024 survey.
sources.title: Gallup — Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports $10.9 billion of acquisition spending by five major public waste companies in 2024, numerous tuck-in deals, treatment and disposal acquisitions, and active acquisition pipelines.
sources.title: Waste Dive — Major Public Waste Companies Spent Nearly $11B on M&A in 2024

sources.supports: Describes an organic-composting model earning tipping fees and compost revenue, with service pricing based on frequency, waste type, weight, volume, and regional market terms and physical processing, curing, and screening.
sources.title: SusGlobal Energy Corp. 2024 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Explains that jurisdictions can mandate recycling and composting and require local governments or contractors to provide services broadly, including universal rollout and service-provider conditions.
sources.title: EPA — Procurement Best Practices: Mandatory Recycling and Composting

sources.supports: Reports 66.2 million tons of wasted food generated by retail, food service, and residential sectors in 2019 with only 5% composted and describes commercial composting process controls, contaminants, scales, and facility types.
sources.title: EPA — Composting

sources.supports: Estimates existing facilities process about 28.3 million tons of residential, commercial, and institutional organic waste and identifies additional infrastructure need plus contamination, land, labor, equipment, permitting, and market barriers.
sources.title: EPA — An Assessment of the U.S. Recycling System: Financial Estimates to Modernize Material Recovery Infrastructure

title: Other Nonhazardous Waste Treatment and Disposal
